critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her treatment of cultural-acquisition opportunities as engineered structural-credentialing instruments whose structural-credentialing outcome justifies the operational and diplomatic-political costs the aggressive acquisition would generate. The same engineered-cultural-instrument-deployment discipline operates across her career as the operational template through which the European-cultural-capital positioning of the regime was constructed.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Walpole collection was transferred to St. Petersburg and integrated into the Hermitage. The British public hostility produced no operational diplomatic consequence. The Walpole acquisition was widely reported across the European cultural community and was treated as the consummation of Catherine's positioning as the major European-cultural patron. The collection became one of the most consequential single acquisitions of the Hermitage's foundational period and is among the most operationally distinctive instances of the engineered-cultural-instrument-deployment discipline applied to the major European-cultural-prestige acquisition domain.

critical_incident_database.decision: In 1764, Catherine founded the Smolny Institute for Noble Maidens — the first state-sponsored educational institution for women in Europe — drawing on the European pedagogical traditions of the Saint-Cyr school of Madame de Maintenon and the broader European convent-school tradition, but extending the institutional architecture to a state-sponsored secular framework that no European monarch had previously deployed.
critical_incident_database.context: Russian female education prior to 1764 was structurally limited to noble-household private instruction with no state-sponsored institutional architecture. The European female-educational architecture was substantially limited to convent-school traditions (Catholic Europe) and household-instruction traditions (Protestant Europe), with the Saint-Cyr school of Madame de Maintenon (founded 1686) as the most significant European precedent for a state-sponsored female-educational institution. The structural opportunity for Catherine was the foundation of a state-sponsored secular institutional architecture that would extend the European precedent and would construct the human-capital foundation for an educated noble-female class. The structural choice was between (a) the conventional Russian default of noble-household private instruction, (b) the foundation of a more limited educational institution within the existing institutional architecture, or (c) the foundation of the structurally-distinctive Smolny Institute as the operational extension of the European precedent to a structurally-novel institutional architecture.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that the human-capital foundation for the broader institutional reform program required the construction of an educated class of nobles whose subsequent operational positioning would supply the administrative apparatus the reforms would require, and that the female-educational dimension was operationally peripheral to the European pedagogical practice but was structurally consequential for the long-arc human-capital construction. She noticed that the Smolny foundation would extend the Catherine educational-infrastructure discipline to a structurally-novel institutional architecture and would construct the institutional precedent for the broader female-educational expansion of subsequent generations.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) construct the human-capital foundation for the broader institutional reform program through the educational-infrastructure construction; (2) extend the European pedagogical precedent to the structurally-novel institutional architecture of the state-sponsored secular female-educational institution; (3) establish the institutional precedent for the broader female-educational expansion of subsequent generations; (4) accept the operational cost of the institutional foundation across the long-arc construction interval as the operational price of the structural-precedent construction.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected the conventional Russian default of noble-household private instruction, which would have foreclosed the human-capital foundation construction. She rejected the foundation of a more limited educational institution within the existing institutional architecture, which would have produced moderate educational-infrastructure outcome without the structural-precedent dimension. She rejected delaying the foundation pending broader institutional-reform progress, which would have left the long-arc human-capital construction operationally compromised across the early-reign period.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Smolny foundation as the long-arc human-capital construction instrument whose continuing operation across multi-decade time horizons would produce the structural-precedent and human-capital outcomes the broader reform program would require. The frame is the same long-arc structural-asset construction frame visible across her career — at the Enlightenment correspondence networks, at the Hermitage acquisitions, at the architectural commissions of imperial St. Petersburg, at the institutional reforms of the 1770s and 1780s.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the Smolny foundation to construct the human-capital foundation for the broader institutional reform program (it did, partially — the institution produced relatively few graduates in the first generation but the institutional precedent extended across subsequent generations); the structural-precedent dimension to compound across the European-cultural reception (it did, with subsequent European female-educational institutions partially emulating the Smolny precedent); the institutional precedent for the broader female-educational expansion to extend across subsequent generations (it did, decisively); and the operational cost of the institutional foundation to be absorbed across the long-arc construction interval (it was).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign undertaking educational reform — would in most cases have either followed the conventional default of noble-household private instruction or founded a more limited educational institution within the existing architecture. Few peers would have executed the structurally-novel institutional architecture that the Smolny foundation represented, treating the female-educational dimension as structurally consequential for the long-arc human-capital construction.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her treatment of the female-educational dimension as structurally consequential for the long-arc human-capital construction and on her willingness to extend the European pedagogical precedent to a structurally-novel institutional architecture. The same long-arc structural-asset construction discipline applied to the educational-infrastructure domain produced the broader educational-infrastructure construction across the reign — the Novodevichy Institute (1765), the Free Economic Society (1765), the Russian Academy (1783), the Statute on Public Schools (1786), and the systematic expansion of the Imperial Academy of Sciences.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Smolny Institute became the institutional precedent for state-sponsored female education across Europe, with subsequent female-educational institutions partially emulating the architecture. The first-generation graduates were relatively few (perhaps 200), but the institutional precedent extended across subsequent generations. The Smolny is the structural origin point of the broader educational-infrastructure construction that operated across the reign as the long-arc human-capital construction instrument, and is one of the most operationally distinctive instances of the long-arc structural-asset construction discipline applied to the educational-infrastructure domain.

critical_incident_database.decision: Following the Polish-partition acquisitions of 1772, Catherine extended operational toleration to the Catholic Jesuit Order in the partition territories — permitting the Jesuits to continue their educational and pastoral operations within the Russian Empire after Pope Clement XIV's suppression of the Order in 1773, when the Jesuits were prohibited elsewhere in Catholic Europe.
critical_incident_database.context: The Polish-partition acquisitions had brought substantial Jesuit institutions and personnel under Russian sovereignty. Pope Clement XIV's brief Dominus ac Redemptor (July 1773) suppressed the Jesuit Order across Catholic Europe, requiring the closure of Jesuit institutions and the dispersal of Jesuit personnel. The structural choice for Catherine was between (a) implementing the papal suppression in the Russian-Polish territories (preserving diplomatic-religious relations with Rome at the cost of the institutional disruption), (b) ignoring the papal brief operationally without explicit toleration declaration (producing operational ambiguity), or (c) the explicit operational toleration that permitted the Jesuits to continue their operations within the Russian Empire.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that the Jesuit institutions in the partition territories provided operational educational and pastoral infrastructure that no Russian alternative could replace at structural-quality, and that the implementation of the papal suppression would produce institutional disruption that the partition-integration project could not absorb. She noticed that the operational toleration would extend the Catherine religious-toleration discipline to a structurally-distinctive case (toleration of a religious order suppressed by its own ecclesiastical superior) that would compound the structural-credentialing of the toleration discipline across the European-religious reception.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) preserve the Jesuit institutional infrastructure in the partition territories for its operational educational and pastoral function; (2) extend the religious-toleration discipline to the structurally-distinctive case of toleration of a religious order suppressed by its own ecclesiastical superior; (3) construct the structural-credentialing outcome that would compound across the European-religious reception; (4) accept the diplomatic-religious cost of operational departure from the papal suppression as the operational price of the institutional preservation and the structural-credentialing outcome.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected implementing the papal suppression in the Russian-Polish territories, which would have produced the institutional disruption that the partition-integration project could not absorb. She rejected operational ambiguity without explicit toleration declaration, which would have produced the operational uncertainty that the institutional preservation required to be resolved. She rejected limiting the toleration to a transitional period pending alternative institutional construction, which would have foreclosed the structural-credentialing outcome.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Jesuit toleration as the operational extension of the religious-toleration discipline to the structurally-distinctive case in which the toleration would preserve operational institutional infrastructure that no Russian alternative could replace at structural-quality. The frame is the same calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment frame visible across her career — at the Old Believer toleration, at the Mennonite-colonist invitation, at the Crimean Tatar integration, at the broader religious-policy management of the imperial structure.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the toleration to preserve the Jesuit institutional infrastructure (it did — the Russian Jesuit Province continued in operation across the suppression period and provided the institutional foundation through which the Jesuit Order would later be restored across Catholic Europe in 1814); the structural-credentialing outcome to compound across the European-religious reception (it did, with the Russian Jesuit Province treated as the operational refuge for Jesuit personnel from across Catholic Europe); the diplomatic-religious cost to be operationally absorbable (it was); and the calibrated-state-management discipline to extend its operational scope through the structurally-distinctive case (it did).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign in the Polish-partition acquisitions — would in many cases have implemented the papal suppression to preserve diplomatic-religious relations with Rome. Few peers would have executed the explicit operational toleration that Catherine deployed, treating the institutional preservation and the structural-credentialing outcome as decisively load-bearing relative to the diplomatic-religious cost.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her treatment of religious policy as a calibrated state-management instrument calibrated by structural-utility rather than as a principled position calibrated by uniform application of doctrinal commitments. The same calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment discipline operates across the religious-policy domain — at the Old Believer toleration, at the Mennonite-colonist invitation, at the Crimean Tatar integration, at the broader religious-policy management — and is one of the operationally distinctive features of her decision-method.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Russian Jesuit Province continued in operation across the suppression period (1773–1814) and provided the institutional foundation through which the Jesuit Order would later be restored across Catholic Europe. The Russian Jesuit Province became the operational refuge for Jesuit personnel from across Catholic Europe across the suppression period. The Jesuit toleration is among the most operationally distinctive instances of the calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment discipline applied to the religious-policy domain and is the operational template through which the broader religious-policy management of the imperial structure was conducted.

critical_incident_database.decision: At the meeting of Mogilev in May–June 1780, Catherine personally negotiated the Russo-Austrian alignment with Joseph II of Austria — establishing the diplomatic architecture that would define European politics through the 1790s, with the alignment supporting Austrian acquiescence in Russian Black Sea expansion in exchange for Russian acquiescence in Austrian Bavarian and Balkan ambitions.
critical_incident_database.context: The Russo-Austrian relationship had been operationally hostile through much of the eighteenth century, with Austria as the traditional ally of the Ottoman Empire against Russian expansion. Joseph II's 1780 ascension to sole rule (after Maria Theresa's death) provided the structural opportunity for the alignment reconstruction. The meeting of Mogilev (May–June 1780) was the operational instrument through which Catherine and Joseph II personally negotiated the alignment, with the negotiations conducted across multiple sessions of substantive diplomatic-strategic exchange. The structural choice for Catherine was between (a) maintaining the conventional Russo-Austrian hostility (preserving the conventional default at the cost of the structural-alignment opportunity), (b) attempting more limited diplomatic alignment through subordinate ministers (producing moderate alignment without the personal-negotiation dimension), or (c) the personal-negotiation discipline that Catherine deployed at Mogilev.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that Joseph II's 1780 ascension provided the operational opportunity for the Russo-Austrian alignment reconstruction, that the personal-negotiation discipline would produce the alignment quality that subordinate-minister negotiation could not match, and that the alignment would provide the European-balance management that the Russian Black Sea expansion would require. She noticed that the structural-asset value of the alignment (Austrian acquiescence in the Crimean annexation, in the Black Sea Fleet construction, in the broader Russo-Turkish strategic settlement) was substantial and would compound across the subsequent decade.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) construct the Russo-Austrian alignment as the European-balance management instrument that the Russian Black Sea expansion would require; (2) deploy the personal-negotiation discipline at Mogilev to produce the alignment quality that subordinate-minister negotiation could not match; (3) construct the integrated diplomatic architecture (Austrian acquiescence in Russian Black Sea, Russian acquiescence in Austrian Bavarian and Balkan ambitions) that would distribute the structural-cost across both partners; (4) accept the operational cost of the personal-negotiation discipline (substantial portions of two months of imperial time) as the operational price of the alignment-quality outcome.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected maintaining the conventional Russo-Austrian hostility, which would have foregone the structural-alignment opportunity and would have left the European-balance management of the Russian Black Sea expansion operationally compromised. She rejected delegating the alignment negotiation to subordinate ministers, which would have produced the alignment-quality that personal negotiation could not match. She rejected limiting the alignment to more bounded operational scope, which would have foregone the integrated diplomatic architecture that the Russo-Turkish strategic settlement would require.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Mogilev meeting as the personal-negotiation deployment of the structural-alignment opportunity that Joseph II's ascension had created. The frame is the same personal-engagement-with-load-bearing-decisions frame visible across her career — at the personal-authoring of the Nakaz, at the personal-authoring of the provincial reform, at the personal-authoring of the Charter to the Nobility, at the personal-deployment of the integrated Potemkin partnership. The disposition is the operational application of the structural-asset construction discipline at the moments when personal engagement is operationally required for the structural-asset construction outcome.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the personal-negotiation to produce the alignment quality that subordinate-minister negotiation could not match (it did); the integrated diplomatic architecture to distribute the structural-cost across both partners (it did); the alignment to provide the European-balance management for the Russian Black Sea expansion (it did — Joseph II's acquiescence in the Crimean annexation of April 1783, his strategic-coordination during the Second Russo-Turkish War of 1787–1792, and the broader Russo-Austrian coordination across the 1780s and 1790s); and the operational cost of the personal-negotiation to be operationally absorbable (it was).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign facing the structural-alignment opportunity — would in many cases have either delegated the negotiation to subordinate ministers or pursued more limited operational alignment. Few peers would have executed the personal-negotiation discipline at the operational scope Catherine deployed at Mogilev, treating the alignment-quality outcome as decisively load-bearing relative to the operational cost of the personal-negotiation discipline.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her treatment of load-bearing diplomatic alignments as requiring personal-negotiation engagement, and on her willingness to deploy substantial portions of imperial time to the personal-negotiation discipline. The same personal-engagement-with-load-bearing-decisions discipline recurs across her career as the operational template through which the structural-asset construction at the moments of operational requirement was conducted.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Russo-Austrian alignment was operationally established at Mogilev and was sustained through the subsequent decade. Joseph II's acquiescence in the Crimean annexation of April 1783, his strategic-coordination during the Second Russo-Turkish War of 1787–1792, and the broader Russo-Austrian coordination across the 1780s and 1790s were the operational outcomes of the Mogilev alignment. The alignment is among the most consequential single diplomatic outcomes of the reign and is one of the operationally distinctive features of her personal-engagement-with-load-bearing-decisions discipline applied to the diplomatic-architecture-construction domain.

critical_incident_database.decision: In June 1790, after the publication of Aleksandr Radishchev's Journey from St. Petersburg to Moscow — a work whose substantive content (criticism of serfdom, exposition of peasant suffering, advocacy of constitutional reform) was structurally consistent with positions Catherine had earlier endorsed in the Nakaz — Catherine ordered Radishchev's arrest, his sentencing to death (commuted to exile in Siberia), and the suppression of the book.
critical_incident_database.context: Radishchev had operated within Catherine's enlightened ambit across the previous two decades, having been educated through Catherine's state-sponsored programs and having served in the Russian customs administration. The Journey was published in May 1790 in St. Petersburg, in approximately 650 copies, with Catherine reading the work shortly after publication. The structural choice for Catherine in June 1790 was between (a) tolerating the publication as consistent with the Nakaz's articulated positions (preserving the performative-Enlightenment positioning at structural-stability cost), (b) discreetly suppressing the publication without prosecution of the author (managing the immediate publication while leaving the precedent unaddressed), or (c) the explicit prosecution and exile that Catherine deployed.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that the post-1789 European environment had operationally shifted in ways that made the continued performative-Enlightenment positioning increasingly incompatible with the structural-stability requirements of the regime, that the Journey's substantive content was structurally consistent with positions Catherine had earlier endorsed but that the post-1789 environment converted the previously-tolerable performative-positioning into operationally-threatening political content, and that the Radishchev prosecution would establish the operational precedent that the post-1789 reactionary turn required. She noticed that the previously-operational channel-bifurcation discipline (continued performative-Enlightenment in European channel paired with operational foreclosure in Russian channel) was no longer operationally feasible in the post-1789 environment because the channel-bifurcation depended on the European-environment continuing to support the performative-Enlightenment positioning.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) establish the operational precedent for the post-1789 reactionary turn through the Radishchev prosecution; (2) suppress the Journey to prevent its operational dissemination across the post-1789 environment; (3) signal to other previously-tolerated Enlightenment-positioned figures (Novikov and his Masonic publishing operations) that the previously-operational toleration was no longer operationally available; (4) accept the substantive contradiction with the Nakaz's articulated positions as operationally required by the post-1789 environment shift.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected tolerating the publication as consistent with the Nakaz's articulated positions, which would have preserved the performative-Enlightenment positioning at structural-stability cost the post-1789 environment had made operationally incompatible. She rejected discreetly suppressing the publication without prosecution of the author, which would have managed the immediate publication while leaving the precedent unaddressed. She rejected limiting the prosecution to a more bounded sanction without exile, which would have foreclosed the operational-precedent dimension that the post-1789 reactionary turn required.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Radishchev prosecution as the operational establishment of the post-1789 reactionary-turn precedent. The frame is the same calibrated-stability-ceiling foreclosure frame visible at the post-Pugachev pivot (operational foreclosure of the peasant-reform direction), with the additional feature that the post-1789 environment shift had operationally foreclosed the channel-bifurcation discipline that had previously sustained the performative-Enlightenment positioning. The Radishchev prosecution is the operational application of the calibrated-stability-ceiling discipline at the moment when the previously-operational instrument (channel-bifurcation) was no longer operationally feasible.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the prosecution to establish the operational precedent for the post-1789 reactionary turn (it did); the suppression to prevent the Journey's operational dissemination (it did, with the work substantially suppressed across the remainder of the reign); the signal to Novikov and other previously-tolerated Enlightenment-positioned figures to be operationally received (it was — Novikov was arrested in 1792); and the substantive contradiction with the Nakaz's articulated positions to be operationally absorbed without producing structural-disruption (it was — the contradiction was the focus of subsequent historiographical criticism but did not produce immediate structural-disruption).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign facing the post-1789 environment shift and the Radishchev publication — would in many cases have either tolerated the publication (with structural-stability cost) or discreetly suppressed without prosecution (managing the immediate publication while leaving the precedent unaddressed). The decision-relevant divergence is the explicit prosecution-and-exile that Catherine deployed, which established the operational precedent for the broader reactionary turn that no more bounded response could have produced.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her recognition that the post-1789 environment shift had operationally foreclosed the channel-bifurcation discipline and required the explicit operational establishment of the reactionary-turn precedent through the Radishchev prosecution. The disposition is the operational application of the calibrated-stability-ceiling discipline at the moment when the previously-operational instrument was no longer operationally feasible, and it is one of the most decision-relevant single instances of the disposition applied to the previously-tolerated Enlightenment-positioned figures.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Radishchev was arrested, was sentenced to death by the Senate court, was commuted to exile in Siberia (specifically, to the Ilimsk fortress in eastern Siberia), and the Journey was substantially suppressed across the remainder of the reign. The prosecution established the operational precedent for the broader post-1789 reactionary turn. Novikov was arrested in 1792 and imprisoned in the Schlüsselburg Fortress. The Russian Masonic lodges were closed in 1792. The censorship system was extended to all foreign publications, with French Revolutionary-period materials specifically targeted. The Radishchev prosecution is the operational pivot of the post-1789 reactionary turn and is the most decision-relevant single instance of the calibrated-stability-ceiling discipline applied to the previously-tolerated Enlightenment-positioned figures.

critical_incident_database.decision: Across 1782–1792, Catherine and Potemkin developed and partially implemented the 'Greek Project' — a long-arc strategic vision for the dismemberment of the Ottoman Empire and the restoration of Constantinople as the capital of a Romanov-Christian Byzantine state, with Catherine's grandson Constantine (named with operational reference to the project) designated as its prospective sovereign.
critical_incident_database.context: The Greek Project emerged in the years following the First Russo-Turkish War as the long-arc strategic framework within which the Russo-Turkish strategic competition would be operationally extended. The project's articulated vision was structurally ambitious: the dismemberment of the Ottoman Empire's European territories (including Constantinople and the Balkan provinces), the restoration of a Romanov-Christian Byzantine state with Constantinople as capital and Constantine as prospective sovereign, the establishment of a Russo-Austrian condominium across the Balkan territories, and the operational extension of Russian strategic projection into the Eastern Mediterranean. The structural choice for Catherine was whether to operate the strategic competition within a more bounded operational framework (warm-water ports, Crimean integration, southern colonization) or within the structurally-ambitious Greek Project framework that the long-arc strategic vision represented.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural opportunity that the Russo-Austrian alignment of 1780 had created for the long-arc strategic-vision framework, the structural-asset value of the operational extension into the Eastern Mediterranean, and the dynastic-positioning value of the Constantine designation as prospective Byzantine sovereign. She noticed that the Greek Project's full operational consummation was unlikely to be achievable within her remaining lifetime but that the long-arc strategic framework would shape Russian strategic positioning across subsequent decades regardless of whether the full consummation was achieved.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) construct the long-arc strategic framework within which the Russo-Turkish strategic competition would be operationally extended; (2) coordinate the Greek Project with the Russo-Austrian alignment to integrate the strategic vision with the diplomatic architecture; (3) deploy the Constantine dynastic-positioning as the symbolic registration of the project's long-arc vision; (4) accept the substantial probability of partial rather than full operational consummation as the operational price of the long-arc strategic-framework construction.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected operating the strategic competition within the more bounded operational framework, which would have foregone the long-arc strategic-vision construction. She rejected articulating the Greek Project without the dynastic-positioning of Constantine, which would have foregone the symbolic-registration dimension. She rejected limiting the project to bilateral Russian construction without the Austrian coordination, which would have foregone the integrated diplomatic architecture and would have invited Austrian counter-mobilization.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Greek Project as the long-arc strategic-vision framework within which the Russo-Turkish strategic competition would be operationally extended. The frame is the same long-arc structural-asset construction frame visible across her career — at the cultural-infrastructure construction (multi-decade accumulation of European-cultural-capital infrastructure), at the institutional reforms (multi-decade construction of the structural-binding architecture), at the educational infrastructure (multi-decade human-capital construction). The Greek Project applies the discipline to the strategic-vision domain, with the long-arc framework constructed even when full consummation is unlikely within the immediate lifetime.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the long-arc strategic framework to be operationally constructed even if the full consummation was unlikely within her remaining lifetime (it was — the project's strategic vision shaped Russian strategic positioning across subsequent decades regardless of the partial-consummation outcome); the Russo-Austrian coordination to integrate the strategic vision with the diplomatic architecture (it did — the 1787–1792 war was prosecuted with substantial Russo-Austrian coordination); the Constantine dynastic-positioning to register the project's long-arc vision (it did, with the Constantine designation being widely understood across the European diplomatic community as referring to the project); and the partial operational consummation to be the realistic outcome (it was — the project was partially advanced through the Russo-Turkish settlements but was not fully consummated, and was substantially abandoned across the post-1789 reactionary turn when the European environment shift made the project's full consummation operationally infeasible).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign engaged in the Russo-Turkish strategic competition — would in most cases have operated the strategic competition within the more bounded operational framework. Few peers would have constructed the long-arc strategic-vision framework that the Greek Project represented, treating the long-arc framework construction as operationally meaningful even when the full consummation was unlikely within the immediate lifetime.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her treatment of long-arc strategic-vision construction as operationally meaningful even when the full consummation is unlikely within the immediate lifetime, and on her capacity to coordinate the strategic-vision construction with the diplomatic architecture and the dynastic positioning. The same long-arc structural-asset construction discipline operates across her career as the operational template through which the structural-asset construction at multi-decade time horizons was conducted.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Greek Project was partially advanced through the Russo-Turkish settlements (the Crimean annexation of 1783, the Treaty of Jassy of 1792 confirming the Russo-Turkish settlement) but was not fully consummated. The post-1789 reactionary turn substantially redirected Russian strategic attention to the European reaction against revolutionary France, foreclosing the operational consummation of the Greek Project within Catherine's lifetime. The project's strategic vision shaped Russian strategic positioning across subsequent decades — informing the Russian role in the Greek War of Independence (1821–1832), the Crimean War (1853–1856), and the Russo-Turkish War of 1877–1878 — without ever being fully consummated. The Greek Project is one of the most operationally distinctive instances of the long-arc strategic-vision construction discipline applied to the strategic-architecture domain across multi-decade time horizons.

critical_incident_database.decision: In April 1792, after the structural escalation of the French Revolution through the September 1792 declaration of the Republic and the early indications of the trajectory that would terminate in the Reign of Terror, Catherine ordered the arrest of Nikolay Novikov — the publisher who had operated extensive Enlightenment publishing operations under Catherine's patronage in the 1770s and early 1780s — and his imprisonment in the Schlüsselburg Fortress on charges related to his Masonic connections, simultaneously closing the Russian Masonic lodges that had operated openly with Catherine's tolerance through the 1770s and 1780s.
critical_incident_database.context: Novikov had been one of the most operationally significant figures of the Russian Enlightenment publishing infrastructure, having published satirical journals (Truten' 1769–1770, Zhivopisets 1772–1773), having operated substantial book-publishing operations through the Free Russian Printing Press and subsequently through the Typographical Company, and having published Enlightenment-philosophical works in Russian translation across the 1770s and 1780s. His operations had been substantially supported by Catherine's tolerance and intermittent patronage. The Russian Masonic lodges had similarly operated openly with Catherine's tolerance through the 1770s and 1780s, with substantial overlap between the Masonic membership and the Enlightenment-publishing personnel. The Novikov arrest and the lodge closure of 1792 were the operational extension of the post-1789 reactionary turn that the Radishchev prosecution of 1790 had established as precedent.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that the post-1789 environment shift had escalated through the September 1792 Republic declaration, that the previously-tolerated Enlightenment-publishing and Masonic operations had become operationally incompatible with the structural-stability requirements of the regime in the post-1789 environment, and that the operational extension of the Radishchev-precedent reactionary turn to Novikov and the Masonic infrastructure was operationally required. She noticed that the previously-operational toleration of Novikov and the Masonic lodges depended on the European-environment continuing to support the performative-Enlightenment positioning, and that the post-1789 environment shift had operationally foreclosed the toleration.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) extend the operational reactionary-turn precedent established by the Radishchev prosecution to Novikov and the Masonic infrastructure; (2) close the institutional infrastructure (publishing operations, Masonic lodges) through which Enlightenment-positioned political content could be operationally disseminated; (3) signal to remaining previously-tolerated Enlightenment-positioned figures that the previously-operational toleration was decisively foreclosed; (4) accept the substantive contradiction with the previous patronage of Novikov as operationally required by the post-1789 environment shift.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected continued tolerance of Novikov's publishing operations and the Masonic lodges, which would have preserved the institutional infrastructure for Enlightenment-positioned political content the post-1789 environment had made operationally threatening. She rejected discrete suppression without prosecution of Novikov, which would have managed the immediate operations while leaving the operational-precedent dimension unaddressed. She rejected limiting the prosecution to a more bounded sanction without imprisonment in the Schlüsselburg, which would have foregone the operational-precedent dimension that the broader reactionary turn required.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Novikov arrest and the Masonic lodge closure as the operational extension of the post-1789 reactionary-turn precedent established by the Radishchev prosecution. The frame is the same calibrated-stability-ceiling foreclosure frame visible across her career — at the post-Pugachev pivot, at the Radishchev prosecution — with the additional feature that the operational extension of the precedent to previously-tolerated figures was operationally required by the post-1789 environment shift's continuing escalation.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the Novikov arrest and the Masonic lodge closure to extend the operational reactionary-turn precedent (it did); the closure of the institutional infrastructure to prevent the operational dissemination of Enlightenment-positioned political content (it did); the signal to remaining previously-tolerated Enlightenment-positioned figures to be operationally received (it was); and the substantive contradiction with the previous patronage of Novikov to be operationally absorbed without producing structural-disruption (it was — the contradiction was the focus of subsequent historiographical criticism but did not produce immediate structural-disruption).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign in the post-1789 environment escalation — would in many cases have either continued the previous tolerance or pursued more bounded operational suppression. The decision-relevant divergence is the explicit prosecution-and-imprisonment that Catherine deployed, which extended the operational reactionary-turn precedent and produced the structural foreclosure of the previously-operational Enlightenment-publishing and Masonic infrastructure.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her recognition that the post-1789 environment shift's continuing escalation required the operational extension of the reactionary-turn precedent to previously-tolerated figures, and on her willingness to deploy the operational extension despite the substantive contradiction with the previous patronage. The disposition is the operational application of the calibrated-stability-ceiling discipline at the moment when the previously-operational toleration was decisively foreclosed by the post-1789 environment shift. The Novikov prosecution is the structural-completion of the post-1789 reactionary turn that the Radishchev prosecution had established as precedent.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Novikov was arrested, was imprisoned in the Schlüsselburg Fortress, and was held until Catherine's death in 1796 (when Paul I released him). The Russian Masonic lodges were closed in 1792 and did not operate openly again until the early years of the nineteenth century. The censorship system was extended to all foreign publications, with French Revolutionary-period materials specifically targeted. The Novikov prosecution and the Masonic lodge closure are the structural-completion of the post-1789 reactionary turn and is among the most decision-relevant instances of the operational extension of the calibrated-stability-ceiling discipline to previously-tolerated previously-patronized figures.

critical_incident_database.decision: In January 1793, Catherine coordinated with Prussia in the Second Partition of Poland — operationally framing the partition as a counter-revolutionary operation to suppress the Jacobinical contagion the Polish Constitution of May 3, 1791 had introduced — and in October 1795 coordinated with Prussia and Austria in the Third Partition that eliminated Poland from the European map.
critical_incident_database.context: The Polish Constitution of May 3, 1791 had abolished the liberum veto, established hereditary monarchy, and constructed institutional reforms structurally similar to the early French Revolution's reforms. The reforms were structurally inconsistent with the post-1789 reactionary positioning Catherine had constructed and were operationally threatening to the long-arc Polish-partition strategy that the First Partition of 1772 had initiated. The structural choice for Catherine was between (a) tolerating the Polish reforms (preserving Polish constitutional autonomy at the cost of the long-arc partition strategy and the post-1789 reactionary positioning), (b) limited intervention to compromise the reforms without further partition (managing the immediate threat without consummating the partition strategy), or (c) the operational extension of the partition strategy through the Second Partition (1793) and the Third Partition (1795) that eliminated Poland from the European map.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that the Polish Constitution's reforms were operationally threatening to the long-arc partition strategy and to the post-1789 reactionary positioning, that the operational opportunity for the structural-completion of the partition strategy was provided by the Polish reform crisis, and that the counter-revolutionary framing of the partition operations would integrate the Polish-partition strategy with the broader post-1789 reactionary positioning. She noticed that the operational extension of the partition strategy through coordinated operations with Prussia (Second Partition) and with Prussia and Austria (Third Partition) would deploy the same complicit-partner cost-distribution mechanism that had operated in the First Partition, with the additional structural-completion dimension that the Third Partition would eliminate Poland from the European map.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) suppress the Polish Constitution's reforms before they could compromise the long-arc partition strategy or extend the Jacobinical contagion to the Russian-Polish territories; (2) consummate the structural-completion of the partition strategy through the Second and Third Partitions that would eliminate Poland from the European map; (3) integrate the Polish-partition operations with the broader post-1789 reactionary positioning through the counter-revolutionary framing; (4) deploy the complicit-partner cost-distribution mechanism through the coordinated operations with Prussia and Austria.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected tolerating the Polish reforms, which would have preserved Polish constitutional autonomy at the cost of the long-arc partition strategy and the post-1789 reactionary positioning. She rejected limited intervention to compromise the reforms without further partition, which would have managed the immediate threat without consummating the partition strategy. She rejected the unilateral Russian intervention without coordinated operations with Prussia and Austria, which would have foregone the complicit-partner cost-distribution mechanism.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Second and Third Partitions as the operational extension of the long-arc partition strategy and the operational integration with the post-1789 reactionary positioning. The frame is the same long-arc structural-completion frame visible across her career — at the Charter to the Nobility (operational completion of the cumulative structural-binding architecture), at the Russo-Turkish strategic settlement (operational completion of the southern empire transformation), at the cultural-infrastructure construction (operational completion of the European-cultural-capital positioning) — with the additional feature that the partition's operational completion would eliminate the structural object (the Polish state) from the European political environment.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the Second and Third Partitions to consummate the structural-completion of the long-arc partition strategy (they did — the Third Partition of 1795 eliminated Poland from the European map until 1918); the integration with the post-1789 reactionary positioning to be operationally effective (it was, with the partitions framed as counter-revolutionary operations and absorbed by the European reactionary environment without the diplomatic-political cost that purely opportunistic partitions would have generated); and the complicit-partner cost-distribution mechanism to operate through the coordinated operations with Prussia and Austria (it did — the partitions were operationally executed without producing the Prussian-Austrian counter-coalition that unilateral Russian intervention would have generated).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign facing the Polish reform crisis — might have either tolerated the reforms (with the long-arc partition strategy compromise) or pursued limited intervention without further partition. Few peers would have executed the structural-completion of the partition strategy that Catherine deployed, treating the operational opportunity for the structural-completion as decisively load-bearing relative to the diplomatic-political cost of the Polish elimination.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her recognition of the structural-completion opportunity that the Polish reform crisis provided, on her integration of the partition operations with the post-1789 reactionary positioning through the counter-revolutionary framing, and on her continued deployment of the complicit-partner cost-distribution mechanism through coordinated operations with Prussia and Austria. The disposition is the operational extension of the long-arc structural-asset construction discipline applied to the structural-completion of the partition strategy.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Second Partition (January 1793) transferred substantial Polish-Lithuanian territory to Russia (most of central Belarus and the western Ukraine) and to Prussia (Greater Poland and Danzig). The Kościuszko Uprising of 1794 was suppressed by Russian and Prussian forces. The Third Partition (October 1795) eliminated Poland from the European map until 1918, with Russia receiving the remaining Lithuanian and Ukrainian territories, Prussia receiving Mazovia and Warsaw, and Austria receiving Western Galicia and Lesser Poland. The partition strategy that began with the First Partition of 1772 was consummated. The Polish elimination is the structural-completion of the long-arc partition strategy and is one of the most operationally distinctive instances of the structural-completion discipline applied to the territorial-strategic domain.

critical_incident_database.decision: Across the early 1790s, Catherine constructed the systematic project of designating her grandson Alexander as successor over her son Paul — separating Alexander and Constantine from Paul's household to be raised at the imperial court under Catherine's personal direction, cultivating Alexander's preparedness for the throne through Frédéric-César de La Harpe's pedagogy, and corresponding with senior advisers (notably Friedrich Melchior Grimm) regarding the operational mechanism for the succession displacement — but died on November 6, 1796 without having issued the formal manifesto designating Alexander as successor.
critical_incident_database.context: Paul Petrovich's structural development across the 1762–1796 period had produced an operational pattern operationally incompatible with the institutional architecture Catherine had constructed: Paul's Prussian-Frederician military aesthetic, his identification with the structural inheritance of Peter III rather than with the institutional reform program, his hostility to the Charter to the Nobility, the provincial reform, and the Russo-Austrian alignment. The structural choice for Catherine across the 1780s and 1790s was whether to permit Paul's accession on her death (with the operational consequence of the institutional-architecture reversal) or to engineer the succession displacement to Alexander (with the operational requirement of formal manifesto designation before her death). The decision-relevant feature of the succession-engineering is not the project's articulation but its operational completion — or, as the historiographical evidence indicates, its operational non-completion at the moment of Catherine's death.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that Paul's accession would produce the operational reversal of the institutional architecture, that Alexander's accession would extend the institutional architecture into subsequent generations, and that the operational mechanism for the succession displacement required the formal manifesto designation before her death. She did not attend, or attended insufficiently, to the operational completion requirement — the formal manifesto was apparently drafted but was not issued before her death, and the structural-engineering brilliance that constructed the institutional architecture of the reign did not extend to the structural-engineering completion of her own succession.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) construct Alexander's preparedness for the throne through the educational separation from Paul's household and through La Harpe's pedagogy; (2) develop the operational mechanism for the succession displacement through correspondence with senior advisers; (3) construct the political support for the succession displacement through the cultivation of Alexander's relationships with the senior aristocracy; (4) defer the operational completion of the succession displacement (the formal manifesto issuance) to subsequent moments at which the procedural conditions would be optimal. The deferral was the operational error — the optimal procedural moment did not arrive before her death.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected accepting Paul's accession on her death, which would have produced the operational reversal of the institutional architecture. She rejected attempting to designate Alexander as successor through informal mechanisms without the formal manifesto, which would have produced operational ambiguity that Paul could have weaponized at the succession moment. She rejected pursuing the formal manifesto issuance at earlier procedural moments when conditions appeared sub-optimal, with the deferral producing the operational non-completion at the moment of her unanticipated death.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the succession-engineering as the long-arc structural-engineering project whose operational completion would be conducted at the optimal procedural moment. The frame is the same long-arc structural-construction frame visible across her career — at the cultural-infrastructure construction, at the institutional reforms, at the diplomatic architecture — with the critical difference that the long-arc construction discipline does not naturally generate the question 'what is the operational completion deadline that constrains the long-arc construction?' For most long-arc construction projects across her career, the deadline was operationally distant and the deferral was operationally absorbable. For the succession-engineering, the deadline was constrained by Catherine's life expectancy and the deferral produced the operational non-completion that the entire project's success depended on avoiding.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the long-arc construction discipline to produce the structural conditions for the succession displacement (it did, partially); the operational completion through the formal manifesto issuance to be conducted at the optimal procedural moment (it was not — the formal manifesto was apparently drafted but was not issued before her death); Paul's accession to be foreclosed (it was not — Paul acceded on November 7, 1796 and reportedly destroyed the displacement manifesto draft); and Alexander's accession to extend the institutional architecture (it did, partially — Alexander I's reign of 1801–1825 partially restored Catherine's institutional architecture after the Pauline interregnum but without the structural integration the original reign had achieved).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign engineering a succession displacement against the legal heir — would in many cases have completed the operational mechanism (formal manifesto issuance, designation of successor in formal succession law) at earlier moments when the conditions were minimally adequate, accepting suboptimal procedural conditions as the operational price of the operational completion. Catherine's deferral of the operational completion in pursuit of optimal procedural moments is the structural-engineering blindness pattern at the limits of her decision-method.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on the structural-engineering blindness at the limits of her decision-method — the long-arc structural-construction discipline that operates effectively across most of her career does not naturally generate the question 'what is the operational completion deadline that constrains the long-arc construction?' For most long-arc construction projects, the deadline is operationally distant and the deferral is operationally absorbable. For the succession-engineering, the deadline is constrained by Catherine's life expectancy and the deferral produced the operational non-completion that destroyed the project's success. The pattern is the same operational-completion blindness visible at Cicero's December 5, 63 BC decision — operational outcome achieved while procedural-precedent dimension left as unresolved structural vulnerability.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Catherine died on November 6, 1796 (Old Style; November 17 New Style) of a stroke without having issued the formal manifesto displacing Paul. Paul, on accession, found in Catherine's papers what he believed to be the draft of the displacement manifesto and reportedly destroyed it (or, in some accounts, it was destroyed by Aleksandr Bezborodko). Paul's reign of 1796–1801 produced the systematic reversal of substantial elements of Catherine's institutional architecture: the Charter to the Nobility was substantially weakened, the Pauline succession law of 1797 introduced strict male-primogeniture rules that operationally foreclosed any future female succession, the Russo-Austrian alignment was reversed, and the institutional architecture of the reign was operationally disrupted. Paul was assassinated on March 23, 1801 by a coterie of senior officers, with Alexander I's reign of 1801–1825 then partially restoring Catherine's institutional architecture. The succession-engineering failure is the most decision-relevant single instance of the structural-engineering blindness at the limits of Catherine's decision-method.

critical_incident_database.decision: Across the 1763–1796 period, Catherine actively recruited Protestant German colonists (notably the Mennonites, beginning with the systematic invitation of 1763 and continuing across the reign) to settle the Volga and the Ukrainian steppes, granting them religious autonomy, exemption from military service, and self-administering local government — extending the calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment discipline to the demographic-colonization domain at scale.
critical_incident_database.context: The Russian southern and Volga steppes in 1763 were operationally underpopulated and were structurally vulnerable to nomadic-frontier instability. The structural choice for the demographic colonization was between (a) the conventional default of Russian peasant resettlement (which would have produced moderate colonization at high social-political cost), (b) limited foreign colonization through bounded invitations to selected populations (which would have produced moderate colonization with manageable structural complications), or (c) the systematic foreign-colonist recruitment that Catherine deployed, with the structural privileges (religious autonomy, military exemption, self-administering local government) calibrated to attract economically-productive populations whose productive contributions to the imperial economy would otherwise have been impossible.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that the Russian southern and Volga steppes required substantial demographic colonization to address the operational-frontier vulnerability and to extend the imperial economic infrastructure, that the conventional Russian peasant resettlement would produce moderate colonization at high social-political cost, and that the systematic foreign-colonist recruitment would produce the structural-asset construction at substantially higher operational quality and at lower social-political cost. She noticed that the structural privileges required to attract the economically-productive populations would compound the calibrated-religious-toleration discipline by extending it to the demographic-colonization domain at scale.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) address the operational-frontier vulnerability of the southern and Volga steppes through systematic demographic colonization; (2) extend the imperial economic infrastructure through the productive contributions of the foreign colonists; (3) deploy the calibrated-religious-toleration discipline at scale through the structural privileges granted to the colonists; (4) accept the operational complications of the foreign-colonist integration (linguistic-cultural distinctness, structural privileges that distinguished the colonists from the surrounding Russian populations) as the operational price of the structural-asset construction.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected the conventional default of Russian peasant resettlement, which would have produced moderate colonization at high social-political cost. She rejected limited foreign colonization through bounded invitations to selected populations, which would have produced moderate colonization without the structural-asset construction at scale. She rejected limiting the structural privileges granted to the colonists, which would have foreclosed the colonist-attraction outcome the systematic recruitment required.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the foreign-colonist recruitment as the operational extension of the calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment discipline to the demographic-colonization domain at scale. The frame is the same calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment frame visible across her career — at the religious toleration policy, at the Polish-partition territories' integration, at the Crimean Tatar integration after the 1783 annexation. The colonist recruitment applies the discipline to the demographic-colonization domain with the structural privileges as the operational instrument calibrated to attract the economically-productive populations.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the systematic foreign-colonist recruitment to produce substantial demographic colonization (it did — by the end of the reign, approximately 30,000 German colonists had settled the Volga, with continuing Mennonite and other colonist settlement across the southern steppes); the structural-asset construction to extend the imperial economic infrastructure (it did, with the colonist communities developing substantial agricultural production and providing the institutional precedent for subsequent demographic colonization); and the operational complications of the foreign-colonist integration to be operationally absorbable (they were, across Catherine's reign — the Volga German communities continued in operation until their deportation by Stalin in 1941).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign undertaking demographic colonization of underpopulated frontier territories — would in most cases have followed the conventional default of internal-population resettlement or pursued bounded foreign-colonist invitations. Few peers would have executed the systematic foreign-colonist recruitment at the scale Catherine deployed, treating the structural privileges as the operational instrument calibrated to attract the economically-productive populations.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her treatment of the demographic-colonization as a calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment problem in which the structural privileges are the operational instrument calibrated by the colonist-attraction outcome. The same calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment discipline operates across her career as the operational template through which the religious-policy, demographic-colonization, and broader structural-population-management of the imperial structure was conducted.
critical_incident_database.outcome: By the end of Catherine's reign, approximately 30,000 German colonists had settled the Volga, with continuing Mennonite and other colonist settlement across the southern steppes. The colonist communities developed substantial agricultural production, provided the institutional precedent for subsequent demographic colonization, and integrated the structural privileges (religious autonomy, military exemption, self-administering local government) into the broader institutional architecture of the empire. The Volga German and Mennonite communities continued in operation until their deportation by Stalin in 1941. The systematic foreign-colonist recruitment is one of the most operationally distinctive instances of the calibrated-state-management-instrument-deployment discipline applied to the demographic-colonization domain at scale.

critical_incident_database.decision: On August 11, 1782 — the centenary anniversary of Peter the Great's accession — Catherine inaugurated the equestrian statue of Peter the Great by Étienne-Maurice Falconet (commissioned by Catherine in 1766, executed across sixteen years, popularly known as the Bronze Horseman) with the inscription 'PETRO PRIMO CATHARINA SECUNDA' ('To Peter the First, Catherine the Second') — operationally constructing the symbolic registration of Catherine as the institutional successor of Peter the Great rather than of the dynastic intermediaries (Anne, Elizabeth, Peter III).
critical_incident_database.context: The Falconet statue commission of 1766 had been the operational extension of the cultural-infrastructure construction discipline to the public-symbolic-registration domain. Falconet, recruited through Diderot's diplomatic mediation, executed the statue across sixteen years with substantial structural difficulty (transport of the 1500-ton 'Thunder Stone' base from the Karelian forest, the technical challenge of the equestrian rearing posture, the multiple iterations of the casting). The inauguration on August 11, 1782 was operationally calibrated to the centenary of Peter the Great's accession (April 27, 1682 according to Old Style — though the inauguration was timed to August for operational reasons related to weather and ceremonial preparation), with the structural symbolic registration of Catherine as Peter's institutional successor through the inscription 'PETRO PRIMO CATHARINA SECUNDA.'

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Catherine attended to the structural fact that the public-symbolic-registration of the regime's institutional positioning required the construction of the major-monument infrastructure that the Falconet statue would provide, and that the inscription 'PETRO PRIMO CATHARINA SECUNDA' would operationally construct the symbolic registration of Catherine as Peter the Great's institutional successor rather than as the displaced wife of Peter III or as a dynastic intermediary in her own right. She noticed that the operational displacement of the dynastic intermediaries (Anne, Elizabeth, Peter III) from the symbolic-registration architecture would compound the legitimacy-construction discipline that had operated since the 1762 coup's Petrine-restoration framing.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Her operative goals were: (1) construct the major-monument infrastructure for the public-symbolic-registration of the regime's institutional positioning; (2) operationally register Catherine as Peter the Great's institutional successor through the 'PETRO PRIMO CATHARINA SECUNDA' inscription; (3) operationally displace the dynastic intermediaries (Anne, Elizabeth, Peter III) from the symbolic-registration architecture; (4) extend the cultural-infrastructure construction discipline to the public-symbolic-registration domain at the structural-quality level the European-cultural-capital positioning required.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: She rejected the more conventional commemorative-monument design that would have produced moderate symbolic-registration without the operational extension to the institutional-successor positioning. She rejected delegation of the inscription design to senior advisers, which would have produced the conventional default rather than the operational displacement of the dynastic intermediaries. She rejected limiting the operational integration with the cultural-infrastructure construction discipline, which would have foregone the structural-credentialing extension of the Falconet commission to the European-cultural reception.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Catherine framed the Falconet statue and its inscription not as a commemorative monument but as the operational instrument through which the symbolic registration of Catherine as Peter the Great's institutional successor would be constructed. The frame is the same engineered-symbolic-registration discipline visible at the 1762 coup (Preobrazhensky uniform of the Petrine era, male riding posture, Petrine-restoration framing of the displacement) and at the broader cultural-infrastructure construction discipline visible across the reign.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: She expected the Falconet statue to construct the major-monument infrastructure (it did — the Bronze Horseman became the canonical visual representation of imperial St. Petersburg and one of the most reproduced equestrian statues in European cultural history); the 'PETRO PRIMO CATHARINA SECUNDA' inscription to operationally register Catherine as Peter the Great's institutional successor (it did — the inscription has been treated by subsequent observers as the canonical formulation of Catherine's institutional positioning); the operational displacement of the dynastic intermediaries to compound the legitimacy-construction discipline (it did, partially — the Pushkin poem 'The Bronze Horseman' of 1833 confirmed the structural reception of the symbolic registration); and the cultural-infrastructure construction extension to the European-cultural reception to be operationally effective (it was, decisively).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another sovereign commissioning a commemorative monument to a predecessor — would in most cases have produced moderate symbolic-registration without the operational extension to the institutional-successor positioning. Few peers would have executed the engineered-symbolic-registration that the 'PETRO PRIMO CATHARINA SECUNDA' inscription deployed, treating the inscription design as an engineered operational instrument rather than as a commemorative formality.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Catherine's divergence rests on her treatment of the Falconet statue and its inscription as engineered operational instruments calibrated for specific symbolic-registration outcomes — the registration of Catherine as Peter the Great's institutional successor and the operational displacement of the dynastic intermediaries. The same engineered-symbolic-registration discipline operates across her career as the operational template through which the symbolic-registration of the regime's institutional positioning was constructed at the moments when public-symbolic-registration was operationally consequential.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The Bronze Horseman became the canonical visual representation of imperial St. Petersburg and one of the most reproduced equestrian statues in European cultural history. The 'PETRO PRIMO CATHARINA SECUNDA' inscription was treated by subsequent observers as the canonical formulation of Catherine's institutional positioning. Pushkin's poem 'The Bronze Horseman' of 1833 confirmed the structural reception of the symbolic registration. The Falconet statue is one of the most operationally distinctive instances of the engineered-symbolic-registration discipline applied to the public-monument domain and is the structural extension of the cultural-infrastructure construction discipline to the European-cultural-capital positioning of imperial St. Petersburg.
